title: poor_password_practices
description: Audit discovers that multiple privileged accounts share the same weak password across systems, and three accounts have no MFA. One account has already been compromised.

timeline.title: Security Audit Finds Shared Weak Passwords
timeline.description: A scheduled security audit reveals that seven privileged accounts across four systems share the same weak password, and three of those accounts have no MFA configured.

timeline.title: Compromised Account Identified
timeline.description: SIEM analysis reveals one of the affected accounts was used to authenticate from an unrecognised foreign IP address 36 hours ago — outside business hours.

timeline.title: Compromised Account Isolated
timeline.description: The compromised account is immediately disabled. All active sessions are terminated and password reset is forced on all related accounts.

timeline.title: Extent of Access Reviewed
timeline.description: Logs for the compromised account are reviewed to determine what resources were accessed and whether any data was exfiltrated or changes made.

timeline.title: MFA Enforced Across All Privileged Accounts
timeline.description: MFA is mandated and enrolled for all privileged accounts. A password manager is deployed and a new password policy enforced organisation-wide.

timeline.title: Password Policy and Hygiene Training Delivered
timeline.description: A revised password policy is communicated to all staff. Targeted training is delivered to system administrators on password hygiene and MFA importance.

node-1.title: Audit Reveals Shared Passwords and Missing MFA
node-1.situation: A scheduled security audit has returned a critical finding: seven privileged accounts across your Active Directory, cloud console, and server management systems share the same eight-character password that does not meet your own policy requirements. Three of these accounts have never had MFA configured. You are reviewing the audit report now. The affected accounts include domain admin, cloud admin, and two database admin accounts.
node-1.question: What is your immediate priority?

options.label: A
options.text: Escalate immediately to the CISO, cross-reference the affected accounts against authentication logs to check for signs of compromise, and begin emergency password resets and MFA enrolment for all affected accounts.

options.label: B
options.text: Schedule a remediation project for the next sprint and notify account owners to update their passwords within two weeks.

options.label: C
options.text: Add the findings to the risk register and review them at the next quarterly security committee meeting.

node-2.title: Active Compromise Discovered
node-2.situation: SIEM review reveals that one of the affected privileged accounts — a domain administrator account — was used to authenticate successfully from a foreign IP address 36 hours ago, at 02:14 AM. Multiple group policy objects were queried and two user accounts were modified during that session. The account is currently inactive but may still have a live credential. You must act immediately.
node-2.question: What is your immediate containment action?

options.label: A
options.text: Immediately disable the compromised account, kill all active sessions, force password resets on all accounts sharing the same password, and isolate any systems where changes were made pending forensic review.

options.label: B
options.text: Reset the compromised account password and monitor to see if the attacker attempts to reconnect.

node-3.title: Assessing the Impact of the Breach
node-3.situation: The compromised account is disabled. Now you must determine the full impact of the 36-hour window of unauthorised access. The session queried group policies and modified two user accounts. You need to understand whether the attacker established persistence or accessed sensitive data.
node-3.question: How do you assess the impact?

options.label: A
options.text: Conduct a full forensic review of all activity during the unauthorised session — including changes to user accounts, group policies, and any data accessed. Audit the two modified accounts for signs of privilege escalation or backdoor creation.

options.label: B
options.text: Revert the two modified user accounts to their previous state and consider the incident contained without further investigation.

options.label: C
options.text: Notify all staff that a breach occurred and ask them to change their passwords as a precaution.

node-4.title: Enforcing Stronger Password and MFA Controls
node-4.situation: The forensic review is complete and no further persistence was established. Leadership now requires a comprehensive remediation of password and MFA practices across the organisation to prevent a recurrence.
node-4.question: Which remediation actions do you implement?

options.label: A
options.text: Mandate MFA for all privileged accounts immediately, enforce a new password policy requiring 16+ characters with complexity, deploy an enterprise password manager, and schedule quarterly privileged account reviews with automated policy compliance checks.

options.label: B
options.text: Send an organisation-wide email reminding staff to use strong, unique passwords and enable MFA where available.

node-5.title: Scenario Complete
node-5.situation: The compromised account was disabled promptly and a thorough forensic review confirmed no persistence was established. All accounts sharing the weak password were reset. MFA has been mandated for all privileged accounts, a strong password policy enforced, and an enterprise password manager deployed. Quarterly privileged account reviews with automated compliance checks are now scheduled.
node-5.question: Scenario complete. Well done!
